claims.text: Argument-winning and problem-solving are mutually exclusive pursuits; the urge to win arguments forces practitioners to make assumptions that are highly dangerous, possibly erroneous, or unfounded.
claims.mechanism: Committing to winning an argument through logical closure (QED) requires accepting premises that may be erroneous; problem-solving requires questioning and revising premises, which argument-winning prohibits.
bounded_by: Applies to contexts where argument-winning is incentivized (academic, debate, higher education settings)
Does not apply to collaborative problem-solving environments without win/lose framing

claims.text: Cultural expansion of higher education has elevated argument-winning over problem-solving as a valued skill, creating systematic bias toward closure and logical victory rather than iterative truth-seeking.
conditions: Time period: contemporary (higher education expansion assumed modern era)
Cultural context: societies with widespread higher education

practices.text: When problem-solving is the goal, deprioritize argument-winning; resist the urge to force QED closure and instead remain open to revising assumptions as new evidence emerges.
